North-star and falsifiable success criteria\n## Overall judgement\n\nThe programme is intellectually serious, unusually candid about its weak evidence base, and worth continuing—but the current design should be revised before it is frozen or converted into experiment beads.\n\nThe architecture literature reviews can begin now. The measurement framework, dependency graph, and several falsifiers should be redesigned first. As written, a W1 “win” could be statistically real yet still depend on resource-band gaming, a narrow and correlated benchmark index, unmatched lifecycle investment, or an inadequately strong neural/RAG baseline.\n\n## 1. Measurement and fair comparison\n\n### What is strong\n\nThe framework in [§1–2](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/programme-3-neurosymbolic-architecture.md:96>) gets several important things right:\n\n- It measures the whole deployed system, not just neural parameters.\n- It freezes stores and indices and includes them in the artifact.\n- It evaluates natural benchmark inputs rather than hand-formalized versions.\n- It requires own-harness baselines, full score vectors, abstention reporting, decontamination, and aligned-store controls.\n- It separates the competitive system claim from kernel-specific attribution.\n- It proposes calibration on neural models before using the instrument.\n\nThose are substantive strengths, not cosmetic safeguards.\n\n### The ±10% matching rule is gameable\n\nBilateral ±10% bands are not the right primary comparison. They permit:\n\n- Padding an artifact to enter a more favorable model band.\n- Avoiding a strong model just outside the band.\n- Having no credible comparator in the intersection of size and compute bands.\n- Calling a system competitive when a smaller or cheaper neural system already dominates it.\n\nReplace bands with a resource-constrained frontier:\n\n> At resource budget `(maximum deployment bytes, maximum latency/energy/memory)`, compare S against the best neural or neural-retrieval system that fits within every budget.\n\nThe comparator should be allowed to be smaller or cheaper, not merely within ±10%. Report interpolation or a staircase envelope across neural Pareto points. W1 should mean that S lies significantly above the best baseline capability frontier, not that it won a favorable neighborhood.\n\nThe claim should also be narrowed from “all other models” to “all pre-registered, reproducible open comparators and baseline families searched under the specified budget.” The literal universal claim is not testable.\n\n### “Same compute” is underspecified and partly mislabeled\n\nKOT-COST/1’s neural-FLOP ledger assigns symbolic work zero. That can be a useful diagnostic—“neural FLOPs”—but it is not a compute ledger. A system performing substantial CPU search, retrieval, decompression, or I/O can appear compute-matched there by definition.\n\nUse a resource vector:\n\n- accelerator time and estimated operations;\n- CPU-seconds;\n- bytes read from storage and network;\n- energy;\n- peak GPU memory and host RSS;\n- end-to-end latency;\n- throughput under a fixed concurrency distribution.\n\nW1 currently mentions wall-clock and memory but omits the energy constraint even though energy is supposedly part of KOT-COST/1. That inconsistency must be fixed.\n\nMeasure both warm- and cold-cache behavior, startup/index-load time, p50/p95 latency, throughput, time to first token, and inter-token latency. Output length must be controlled or cost normalized appropriately. MLPerf uses explicit latency, throughput, compliance, and full-system power methodology for exactly these reasons; the programme should borrow that measurement discipline rather than relying on query-level `nvidia-smi` readings alone. [MLPerf inference methodology](https://mlcommons.org/benchmarks/inference-datacenter/), [MLPerf power measurement](https://docs.mlcommons.org/inference/power/).\n\n### Deployment size is useful, but not sufficient\n\nRaw on-disk bytes are a defensible requested metric, provided packaging is canonical. Otherwise serialization format, padding, duplicate metadata, compression settings, and what gets moved into the “common base image” are gaming surfaces.\n\nReport at least:\n\n1. Canonically packed, minimally sufficient artifact bytes.\n2. Compressed distribution bytes.\n3. Warm resident RAM/VRAM.\n4. Cold-start working set and bytes read.\n5. Index/store construction size and cost.\n6. Any remote service, cache, or corpus dependency.\n\nFreeze the common base image before architecture development. Otherwise custom functionality can migrate into the supposedly free base.\n\nMost importantly, add a lifecycle ledger. The current design explicitly compares deployment efficiency, not equal total compute. That is legitimate, but it must be named accurately. Report:\n\n- donor pretraining provenance where known;\n- architecture fine-tuning and hyperparameter-search compute;\n- store authoring, parsing, embedding, indexing, and human-review costs;\n- total cost amortized at several deployment volumes, such as \\(10^3, 10^6,\\) and \\(10^9\\) queries.\n\nTraining longer to obtain a smaller model is a real deployment strategy, and optimal model size depends on expected inference volume. [Sardana et al., “Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal”](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v235/sardana24a.html).\n\n### Baselines need strengthening\n\nThe strongest missing control is:\n\n> Same corpus, same retriever/index, same resource budgets, but a conventional neural RAG or tool-use architecture.\n\nThe aligned-non-kernel store does not replace this. The programme must distinguish:\n\n- kernel semantics;\n- structured storage;\n- retrieval;\n- deterministic execution;\n- retry/search;\n- specialized neural-symbolic integration.\n\nBaseline optimization should include quantization, structured pruning, distillation, retrieval, and compute-optimal sampling or verification. Best-of-\\(k\\) self-consistency alone is too weak: adaptive test-time compute can substantially outperform naive best-of-\\(N\\). [ICLR 2025 test-time-compute study](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/hash/1b623663fd9b874366f3ce019fdfdd44-Abstract-Conference.html).\n\nEqual numbers of hyperparameter configurations are also not sufficient symmetry because configurations differ greatly in cost. Match total tuning compute and use fixed selection rules.\n\n### Decontamination is necessary but cannot certify fairness\n\nN-gram and embedding overlap screening can catch gross answer-key leakage, but not:\n\n- benchmark source knowledge encoded as rules or abstractions;\n- selective store authoring based on benchmark error analysis;\n- paraphrases below the threshold;\n- benchmark contamination in donor weights;\n- repeated researcher adaptation to the frozen test suite.\n\nAdd a sealed evaluation component produced after architecture and store freeze, preferably by an independent party or procedural generator. For store-based systems, include temporally fresh facts or independently held-out domains.\n\nUse a factorial control design: content type × retrieval architecture × executor. Derangement alone is weak because it changes alignment and often retrieval behavior simultaneously. Include label permutation, irrelevant-but-structurally-matched records, edge shuffling, and matched generic RAG.\n\n### Statistical specification needs correction\n\nW1 should require the lower confidence bound on the difference to exceed the practical margin:\n\n\\[\nLCB_{95}(INDEX(S)-INDEX(B)) > \\delta_k\n\\]\n\nA point estimate above \\(\\delta_k\\) plus an LCB merely above zero does not establish the margin.\n\nBecause S is tested against multiple baselines, use simultaneous confidence bounds or family-wise error control. Bootstrap hierarchically across benchmark families and items, preserving paired predictions.\n\n“TOST non-inferiority” is technically incorrect. TOST is normally an equivalence procedure with two one-sided tests. A non-inferiority claim uses the appropriate single one-sided test or confidence bound against the non-inferiority margin.\n\n## The AI index\n\nThe current nine-task suite is reasonable for SmolLM2 harness calibration. It is not adequate as the headline “AI-capability index.”\n\nSeven components are closely related multiple-choice commonsense or factual tasks. An unweighted macro-average therefore overweights that construct while underrepresenting:\n\n- language modeling and linguistic competence;\n- instruction following;\n- natural-language generation;\n- compositional and relational generalization;\n- logical reasoning;\n- calibration and selective prediction;\n- multilingual performance;\n- coding below R-3;\n- robustness under distribution shift.\n\nThe raw average also combines binary, four-way, five-way, and open-ended tasks with different chance floors and variances. Normalize scores relative to chance and a pinned ceiling, then macro-average first within capability domains and only then across domains. Publish both the domain vector and the scalar.\n\nMMLU-cloze is particularly problematic: the repository itself notes inconsistent MMLU variants across SmolLM2 sizes. It should not be silently treated as a stable cross-rung component.\n\nRecommended structure:\n\n- **R-0:** held-out language-model loss plus BabyLM-style BLiMP/EWoK linguistic evaluation; small procedural relational and rule-reasoning suites.\n- **R-1/R-2 general capability:** a reduced set from HellaSwag, ARC-Challenge, PIQA, WinoGrande, MMLU, GSM8K, plus instruction following where above floor.\n- **Neurosymbolic diagnostics:** CLUTRR, ProofWriter/RuleTaker, FOLIO or similar, with held-out rules, relation compositions, proof depths, and lexical paraphrases. CLUTRR explicitly evaluates systematic relational generalization and includes natural-language graph construction, making it especially relevant. [CLUTRR paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.06177).\n- **R-3+:** MMLU-Pro, BBH or BBEH where discriminative, IFEval, code, and a frozen release of LiveBench or another recent objective benchmark.\n- **Contamination-resistant secondary:** a sealed or periodically refreshed suite. LiveBench is useful because it refreshes questions and uses objective scoring, though its changing nature means each release must be versioned rather than folded silently into a fixed index. [LiveBench](https://github.com/livebench/livebench).\n\nHELM’s multi-scenario, multi-metric structure is a better conceptual model than a flat accuracy average. [Stanford HELM](https://nlp.stanford.edu/helm/vhelm/). The Open LLM Leaderboard v2 suite is suitable at larger scales but the document is right that much of it may floor out at tiny scales. [Official benchmark descriptions](https://huggingface.co/docs/leaderboards/main/open_llm_leaderboard/about).\n\nKeep the current suite as “SmolLM2 continuity index,” not as the sole capability index.\n\n## 2. Architecture bets\n\n### Verifier loop\n\nThis is the best-motivated family because it has the only measured system-level positive shape. The proposed index-level falsifier is sensible, provided it compares against:\n\n- compute-optimal neural sampling;\n- a learned neural verifier;\n- generic structured RAG;\n- grammar/constrained decoding;\n- the same verifier with a non-kernel store.\n\nRun a coverage upper-bound calculation first:\n\n\\[\n\\Delta_{\\text{maximum index}} \\leq \\sum_b w_b \\kappa_b\n(1-\\text{baseline accuracy on covered items})\n\\]\n\nIf even perfect covered-item correction cannot reach \\(\\delta\\), kill the index-mover claim without GPU work.\n\n### GNN fusion\n\nThe hypothesis is plausible, particularly for relational composition and systematic generalization. But “GNN ≤ text serialization” at R-0/R-1 does not kill the entire family. Text is often superior for tiny graphs, while graph encoders may become useful only as graph size and reasoning depth grow.\n\nControls should include:\n\n- same retrieved nodes with no edges;\n- shuffled edges and relation labels;\n- MLP/DeepSets/Transformer encoders with the same inputs;\n- text serialization at equal information and resource budgets;\n- held-out relation compositions and proof depths;\n- oracle graph versus NL-extracted graph.\n\nSeparate four stages: retrieval, graph construction, graph encoding, and LM use. A positive delivery probe is insufficient; require causal behavioral improvement.\n\n### Rule placement\n\nThe cheap-first order CD → KV → adapters/activations → hidden layer is sensible. Several claims need tightening:\n\n- Constrained decoding does not itself perform inference; it enforces an output language unless the external engine has already derived the valid continuation set.\n- Attention weights are not adequate provenance evidence. KV provenance requires causal ablation, intervention, or executor traces.\n- A dedicated concept bottleneck is not strongly interpretable merely because its units are named. Provenance must be causally validated.\n- Cost parity with the external loop should not be the only endpoint. An internal mechanism might be worthwhile if it improves accuracy, coverage, or latency variance. Compare Pareto frontiers.\n\nA promising missing design is explicit NL-to-program synthesis: parse into a small executable DSL, execute deterministically, and generate from the checked result with calibrated abstention. That attacks the actual boundary problem more directly than hoping rule knowledge emerges in a hidden layer.\n\n### Ground-up training\n\nA matched twin is correct, but one R-0 failure should not imply failure at R-2. Interfaces may require enough model capacity to become usable. Run at least three small widths or token budgets and test the direction of scaling.\n\nThe most promising training variants missing are:\n\n- distillation from symbolic proof traces;\n- verifier-guided training rather than verifier-only retry;\n- synthetic curriculum over increasing rule depth and paraphrase diversity;\n- learned routing so the symbolic path is activated selectively;\n- conditional-compute or MoE designs with a symbolic expert.\n\n### Dimension drop\n\nThis is currently the weakest technical component.\n\nAn arbitrary per-layer orthogonal rotation is not generally function-preserving through elementwise nonlinearities, normalization, residual connections, attention head structure, and tied embeddings merely because adjacent linear maps are adjusted. Some restricted residual-stream basis changes can be absorbed, but the exact invariance group must be derived for the chosen architecture.\n\nSAE features are typically overcomplete and non-orthogonal; matching them to concepts does not produce a removable orthogonal coordinate block.\n\nAlso, dropping 80% of hidden width does not imply a 5× smaller dense transformer. Major matrices scale approximately quadratically with width, so the reduction could be much larger—while embeddings and other components scale differently. Conversely, “dropping 80% of dimensions” could mean rank or parameter pruning, in which case 5× needs an explicit parameterization.\n\nBefore the five-arm full experiment, add a cheaper causal gate:\n\n1. Identify a purported concept subspace.\n2. Ablate or patch it on concept-relevant and irrelevant tasks.\n3. Test stability across layers, prompts, and donors.\n4. Show that store reinjection specifically restores the lost behavior.\n5. Only then attempt structural width pruning and distillation.\n\nCompare against the strongest retrained structured-pruning and knowledge-distillation baselines, not pruning without recovery training.\n\n## 3. Scaling ladder and cost approach\n\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

This is the anti-l3a/a5 rule: the parser is\n  part of the product, and its failures are the system's failures [STIPULATED:\n  ASM-0808].\n\n### 1.2 Size — KOT-SIZE/2 (the six-figure size protocol)\n\nRaw on-disk bytes remain the maintainer-directed primary, but packaging is a gaming\nsurface (serialization format, padding, duplicated metadata, what migrates into the\n\"free\" base image) [STIPULATED: ASM-0810, answering review \"Deployment size is\nuseful, but not sufficient\"].\n\n- DECISION: size is reported as SIX pinned figures, with figure (1) primary:\n  (1) **canonically-packed, minimally-sufficient artifact bytes** — a pinned\n  canonical packing script (deterministic serialization, no padding, deduplicated\n  metadata) applied identically to every arm; (2) compressed distribution bytes\n  (zstd-19, pinned level); (3) warm resident RAM/VRAM while serving; (4) cold-start\n  working set + total bytes read to first answer; (5) index/store construction\n  size and construction cost (feeds KOT-LIFE/1); (6) any remote service, cache, or\n  corpus dependency — a system with a remote dependency CANNOT claim a smaller\n  deployment than one without it; remote bytes count or the claim is void\n  [STIPULATED: ASM-0810].\n- DECISION: the **common base image is FROZEN (content-hashed) before any\n  architecture development begins** — P3-D-INDEX's first deliverable; anything a\n  system needs beyond the frozen image counts as its bytes. This closes the\n  \"migrate custom functionality into the free base\" channel the review names\n  [STIPULATED: ASM-0810].\n\nThere is no \"the kernel is free\" accounting, ever. Quantisation games are\nsymmetric: if the neurosymbolic arm quantises, every comparator gets the same\nbest-effort quantisation (via the frontier-builder, §2.3).\n\n### 1.3 Inference cost — KOT-COST/2 (the resource vector) + the neural-FLOP diagnostic\n\nThe review's technical error #7 is accepted: a ledger in which symbolic\ncomputation enters at zero is not a compute measure — a system doing substantial\nCPU search, retrieval, decompression, or I/O could look \"compute-matched\" by\ndefinition. Revision 1's L1 is therefore RENAMED and DEMOTED to what it actually\nis, and the binding ledger becomes a full resource vector [STIPULATED: ASM-0810].\n\n- DECISION: inference cost is the measured **resource vector KOT-COST/2**, per\n  query and per suite, on pinned hardware under the P3-D-HW repeatability\n  protocol: (a) accelerator time + estimated accelerator ops; (b) CPU-seconds;\n  (c) bytes read from storage and network; (d) energy (RAPL on CPU,\n  NVML/`nvidia-smi`-class counters on GPU, whole-run integration — not query-level\n  spot reads); (e) peak accelerator memory AND host RSS; (f) end-to-end latency at\n  p50 and p95; (g) throughput under a pinned concurrency distribution; (h) warm-\n  and cold-cache conditions both measured, including startup/index-load time;\n  (i) time-to-first-token and inter-token latency for generative arms; (j) output\n  length controlled (pinned max-token + stop discipline) or cost normalised per\n  emitted token, pinned per benchmark. Methodology borrows MLPerf inference +\n  power measurement discipline (REVIEW-CITED: MLPerf inference-datacenter\n  benchmarks + MLPerf power methodology; verified and operationalised by\n  P3-LR-SYS → P3-D-HW before the framework freezes) [STIPULATED: ASM-0810].\n- DECISION: the analytic neural-FLOP ledger survives as the **neural-FLOP\n  diagnostic** (2·params·tokens + attention terms, pinned formula sheet): useful\n  to keep the neural parts honest and hardware-independent, REPORTED always,\n  BINDING never — no \"compute-matched\" claim may cite it alone [STIPULATED:\n  ASM-0810; corrects technical error #7].\n\nPrecedent that the symbolic side can be genuinely cheap on the measured vector —\nand that the accounting has to be measured, not assumed:\n\n- PREMISE: the deterministic engine answers covered queries at 5.29–7.82 µs/query,\n  and the f2b system-level cost ratio vs the 1.7B host was measured at 0.103\n  [MEASURED: registry/verdicts/l3a.json + registry/verdicts/a5.json engine timing;\n  registry/verdicts/f2b-replicate.json cost_ratio_vs_R3].\n\n### 1.3a The lifecycle ledger — KOT-LIFE/1\n\nThe comparison this programme runs is a *deployment-efficiency* comparison, not an\nequal-total-investment comparison — legitimate, but it must be named accurately\nand priced [STIPULATED: ASM-0810, answering review \"add a lifecycle ledger\"].\n\n- DECISION: every W1-relevant system (S and every comparator) publishes a\n  **lifecycle ledger**: (1) donor pretraining provenance and compute where known\n  (dimension-drop arms *inherit* their donor's training compute — stated, never\n  netted out); (2) architecture fine-tuning + hyperparameter-search compute\n  actually spent (the tuning-symmetry audit trail, §2.3); (3) store costs:\n  authoring, parsing, embedding, indexing, and human review, in hours and $;\n  (4) total cost of ownership amortised at three pinned deployment volumes —\n  10^3, 10^6, 10^9 queries — using the measured KOT-COST/2 energy/latency figures.\n  Training longer to obtain a smaller model is a real deployment strategy and\n  optimal size depends on inference volume (REVIEW-CITED: Sardana et al., \"Beyond\n  Chinchilla-Optimal\"; verified by P3-LR-SYS). The ledger CO-REPORTS with every W1\n  claim and feeds kill condition K-P3v2(6); it does not gate W1 numerically\n  because store-authoring hours and GPU-hours have no non-arbitrary common unit —\n  the honesty mechanism is mandatory publication + the kill condition, not a\n  fudged exchange rate [STIPULATED: ASM-0810].\n\n### 1.4 The exact win condition and the exact shrink objective\n\nThe revision-1 ±10% band construction is WITHDRAWN as gameable (padding into a\nfavourable band; dodging a strong model just outside it; empty band\nintersections; losing to a smaller/cheaper dominator while \"winning the\nneighbourhood\") [STIPULATED: ASM-0810, accepting review ranked concern #1 in\nfull].\n\n**W1 (the resource-frontier competitive claim), verbatim:** at rung R-k, with a\npre-registered resource budget vector\n`B_k = (max canonically-packed deployment bytes; max p95 latency; max energy/query;\nmax peak accelerator memory + host RSS)`, a Programme-3 system S beats the\nbaseline frontier iff, under the frozen KOT-AI-INDEX/2 version for that rung,\nown-harness:\n\n1. **Admissibility:** S fits within EVERY component of B_k, measured under\n   KOT-SIZE/2 + KOT-COST/2, warm and cold conditions per the P3-D-HW protocol\n   (energy explicitly binds here — fixing revision 1's omission of energy from\n   W1 while KOT-COST claimed it); and\n2. **Frontier dominance:** for EVERY comparator C in the pre-registered comparator\n   set F(B_k) — every pre-registered, reproducible open comparator and baseline\n   family that fits within every component of B_k, explicitly INCLUDING systems\n   smaller or cheaper than S, each optimised for the budget by the frontier-builder\n   (§2.3) — the simultaneous one-sided 95% lower confidence bound satisfies\n   `LCB95(INDEX(S) − INDEX(C)) > δ_k`, with family-wise error control across the\n   comparator set and a hierarchical bootstrap over benchmark families and items\n   preserving paired predictions (§2.5); and\n3. the decontamination audit (§2.2) AND the sealed-evaluation consistency gate\n   (§2.2a) pass; and\n4. the factorial controls (§2.2) behave as pre-registered (derangement/permutation\n   destroys any store-content-attributed component; the aligned-non-kernel and\n   matched-generic-RAG results are REPORTED as the attribution split, whatever\n   they show); and\n5. the lifecycle ledger (§1.3a) is published alongside.\n\nReporting: the full **staircase/Pareto envelope** of neural and neural-retrieval\npoints across budgets (index vs bytes, index vs cost) is published with S plotted\nagainst it — W1 means S lies significantly above the envelope at its budget, not\nthat it won a favourable neighbourhood.\n\n**Scope of the claim licensed by a W1 PASS, verbatim:** \"S exceeds every\npre-registered, reproducible open comparator and baseline family searched under\nbudget B_k by ≥ δ_k on KOT-AI-INDEX/2-vN\" — NEVER \"higher than all other models of\nthe same size and compute\"; the literal universal is untestable and is hereby\nretired from every claim surface [STIPULATED: ASM-0810].\n\n**W2 (the shrink objective), verbatim:** once W1 first holds at any rung, minimise\nKOT-SIZE/2 figure-(1) bytes subject to `INDEX(S) ≥ INDEX(M_ref)` where M_ref is a\nnamed, pinned open reference model (initial M_ref = SmolLM2-1.7B own-harness;\nrevisited by maintainer fork per index version). answers review \"Use a factorial control\n   design\"].\n\n### 2.2a The sealed evaluation (new; P3-D-SEAL)\n\nDecontamination screening is necessary but CANNOT certify fairness: it misses\nbenchmark-source knowledge encoded as rules/abstractions, selective store\nauthoring guided by benchmark error analysis, paraphrases below threshold,\ncontamination already in donor weights, and repeated researcher adaptation to the\nfrozen suite (the review's list, adopted verbatim) [STIPULATED: ASM-0812].\n\n- DECISION: every W1 claim additionally requires a **sealed evaluation**: a\n  secondary suite produced AFTER architecture + store freeze, by an independent\n  party or a pinned procedural generator, unseen by every developing agent;\n  for store-based systems it includes temporally-fresh facts and/or independently\n  held-out domains. The sealed suite is versioned per release (LiveBench-style\n  refresh discipline — each release a new version, never silently folded in). Gate\n  shape: the sealed-suite result must be directionally consistent with the frozen\n  suite (pre-registered consistency band); a large frozen-vs-sealed gap voids the\n  win pending diagnosis [STIPULATED: ASM-0812].\n\n### 2.3 Confound C — baseline strength, the frontier-builder, and tuning symmetry\n\n- DECISION: the comparator set F(B_k) is built by a standing **neural\n  frontier-builder** (P3-D-FRONTIER): for each pre-registered budget B_k it\n  produces the strongest baselines that fit, drawing on (i) the strongest open\n  pure-neural models at or below budget (pinned name+revision at freeze);\n  (ii) budget-optimising transforms — quantisation, structured pruning,\n  distillation — applied best-effort to those models; (iii) **neural + retrieval:\n  a conventional RAG / tool-use architecture over the SAME corpus, SAME\n  retriever/index, SAME budgets as the kernel arm** (the strongest missing control\n  — the aligned-non-kernel store does NOT replace it); (iv) optimised/adaptive\n  test-time compute — best-of-k self-consistency alone is too weak; the builder\n  implements compute-optimal adaptive strategies (REVIEW-CITED: the ICLR-2025\n  test-time-compute result; verified by P3-LR-RAG/P3-LR-EVAL) and learned/neural\n  verifiers where budget permits; (v) training-compute-matched from-scratch twins,\n  required only where the neurosymbolic arm itself trains from scratch (H-GU)\n  [STIPULATED: ASM-0812].\n- DECISION: the six-way attribution factorisation is a standing analysis\n  requirement: any observed win must be decomposed as far as the control cells\n  allow across **kernel semantics vs structured storage vs retrieval vs\n  deterministic execution vs retry/search vs neural-symbolic integration** — the\n  factorial cells in §2.2(2) + the RAG control are exactly the instruments for\n  this [STIPULATED: ASM-0812].\n- DECISION: tuning symmetry binds on **total tuning compute** (accelerator-hours +\n  CPU-hours actually spent on search, logged per arm into KOT-LIFE/1), with a\n  FIXED, pre-registered selection rule on a dev split disjoint from the index\n  suite — equal config counts are NOT sufficient (configs differ enormously in\n  cost). An architecture that only wins with more search compute has not won\n  [STIPULATED: ASM-0812; corrects revision 1's config-count rule].\n\n### 2.4 What P3-MF-0 must deliver (the framework as an artifact)\n\nA frozen `KOT-FAIR/2` spec: the threat model (§2.0); index suite pins + domain\nstructure + normalisation constants (§1.1); the KOT-SIZE/2 canonical packing\nscript + six-figure recipe + frozen base image hash; the KOT-COST/2 measurement\nrig + the P3-D-HW hardware-repeatability and warm/cold protocol; the KOT-LIFE/1\nledger template; the decontamination screener; the sealed-eval production\nprotocol; the frontier-builder spec + comparator pinning rule; the statistical\nanalysis plan (§2.5); and a **calibration report**: the full framework run on\npure-neural models ONLY (135M vs 360M vs 1.7B), demonstrating that the index\nseparates known-ordered models with the expected ordering and stable variance,\nand that the cost rig reproduces across days/reboots within its pre-registered\nrepeatability band — the instrument is validated on cases with known answers\nbefore it ever judges a neurosymbolic system. Per governance, nothing here is ingested into the KB — the staging\n    47\t  file `docs/next/lit/EVAL.sources.jsonl` is for the coordinator's central\n    48\t  ingest.\n    49\t- `docs/next/eval-necessity-adjudication.md`, `docs/research-plan/08-stats-*` —\n    50\t  provenance-triple + one-primary discipline, inherited not restated.\n    51\t\n    52\tWhat is NEW here: HELM's construction and aggregation; the OLLv2 component set,\n    53\tnormalization mathematics, and measured tiny-scale floors; contamination\n    54\tdetection limits + how sealed benchmarks are actually produced; proxy-rung\n    55\tvalidity (the metric-dependence result); per-benchmark floor/ceiling evidence\n    56\tfor the KOT-AI-INDEX/2 D1–D7 table; and the four Phase-1 design hand-offs.\n    57\t\n    58\t---\n    59\t\n    60\t## 1. HELM: multi-scenario construction, and why a flat accuracy average is inadequate\n    61\t\n    62\t- LOAD-BEARING: HELM (Liang, Bommasani, Lee et al., \"Holistic Evaluation of\n    63\t  Language Models\", TMLR 2023) evaluates 42 scenarios (16 core + 26 targeted)\n    64\t  under 7 metrics per core scenario — accuracy, calibration, robustness,\n    65\t  fairness, bias, toxicity, efficiency — with standardized conditions that\n    66\t  raised cross-model scenario coverage from 17.9% to 96.0% over 30 models\n    67\t  [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2211.09110, TMLR 2023].\n    68\t- The construction the review told us to copy is the two-level structure:\n    69\t  scenarios (use cases) × metrics (desiderata), taxonomized first and THEN\n    70\t  populated, so gaps are explicit rather than invisible. A flat accuracy\n    71\t  average has no seat for calibration/robustness at all, and mixes scenarios\n    72\t  with different chance floors and variances into one number — HELM's central\n    73\t  argument is that single-metric evaluation lets \"metrics beyond accuracy fall\n    74\t  to the wayside\" and hides trade-offs [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2211.09110, 2023].\n    75\t- Aggregation caution: HELM's original headline aggregate was the **mean win\n    76\t  rate** (fraction of head-to-head comparisons won across scenarios). This is\n    77\t  ORDINAL — it obscures effect magnitude, and it is comparator-set-dependent:\n    78\t  adding/removing models changes every score, and small scenario-score\n    79\t  variations can invert ranks. Later HELM-family work moved to mean score over\n    80\t  normalized metrics for exactly this reason. **UNVERIFIED at primary source**\n    81\t  for the internal aggregation details (read via secondary sources — the AHELM\n    82\t  paper and topic surveys; the HELM paper abstract itself was fetched but its\n    83\t  aggregation section was not) — carried as a flag, not a fact.\n    84\t- DECISION for P3-D-INDEX [STIPULATED]: adopt HELM's *structure* (domains ×\n    85\t  metrics, gaps explicit, vector always published) but NOT mean-win-rate\n    86\t  aggregation — a win-rate scalar would make KOT-AI-INDEX/2 comparator-set\n    87\t  dependent, which directly conflicts with W1's pre-registered frontier F(B_k)\n    88\t  (adding a comparator must not move S's own index). Use chance/ceiling\n    89\t  normalization + macro-averaging (§2), which is absolute per system.\n    90\t\n    91\t## 2. Composite-index statistics: normalization, macro-averaging, and when a scalar is defensible\n    92\t\n    93\t**Normalization against chance (the practice standard).**\n    94\t\n    95\t- LOAD-BEARING: the Open LLM Leaderboard v2 normalizes every score by\n    96\t  subtracting the task's random-guess baseline and rescaling to 0–100:\n    97\t  `norm = (raw − lower_bound)/(1 − lower_bound)`, clamped to 0 below chance;\n    98\t  for tasks with heterogeneous subtasks (BBH's 24 subtasks with num_choices\n    99\t  2–19; MuSR's 2/5/3-choice subtasks) each subtask is normalized against ITS\n   100\t  OWN chance floor first and the normalized scores are then averaged;\n   101\t  generative tasks (MATH, IFEval) take lower_bound = 0 [LIT-BACKED:\n   102\t  huggingface.co/docs/leaderboards/open_llm_leaderboard/normalization, 2024].\n   103\t- This verifies the exact shape ASM-0811 step (1)–(2) stipulated: normalize\n   104\t  within-benchmark against chance, THEN average — with one difference:\n   105\t  **OLLv2 pins the ceiling at 1.0; KOT-AI-INDEX/2 additionally pins a\n   106\t  benchmark-specific ceiling** (human/large-reference). No published leaderboard\n   107\t  we verified does ceiling-pinning; that piece is ours to freeze and defend\n   108\t  [STIPULATED]. The candidate ceilings this review verified: BLiMP human 88.6%\n   109\t  (individual) / 96.4% (aggregate) [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1912.00582, TACL 2020];\n   110\t  EWoK human > all tested models 1.3B–70B (exact human % in paper body, not\n   111\t  re-verified here — pin at freeze) [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2405.09605, TACL 2025].\n   112\t  Where no human ceiling exists, ASM-0811's \"named pinned reference model\"\n   113\t  fallback stands.\n   114\t- Different answer cardinalities are therefore handled (per-subtask floors),\n   115\t  but **different variances are NOT handled by OLLv2** — a 250-item benchmark\n   116\t  and a 14k-item benchmark enter the average with equal weight and very\n   117\t  different standard errors. The §2.5 hierarchical bootstrap (families → items,\n   118\t  paired) is the correct treatment and has no off-the-shelf leaderboard\n   119\t  precedent we could verify; it must be specified in the P3-D-INDEX analysis\n   120\t  plan [STIPULATED].\n   121\t\n   122\t**Is a scalar defensible at all?**\n   123\t\n   124\t- LOAD-BEARING: metabench (Kipnis et al., ICLR 2025) fit IRT models over\n   125\t  ~5,000 public LLMs × 28,632 items from six benchmarks (ARC, GSM8K, HellaSwag,\n   126\t  MMLU, TruthfulQA, WinoGrande) and found (a) <3% of items reconstruct each\n   127\t  benchmark score at 1.24% RMSE and the total at 0.58% RMSE, and (b) a single\n   128\t  common factor with Spearman r = 0.94 against the total score [LIT-BACKED:\n   129\t  arXiv:2407.12844, ICLR 2025].\n   130\t- Reading for us [STIPULATED]: the six legacy benchmarks are so redundant that\n   131\t  their average measures ONE factor — which is precisely the GPT-5.6 objection\n   132\t  to revision-1's nine-task suite (seven correlated MC tasks). A scalar over a\n   133\t  suite dominated by one factor is defensible but UNINFORMATIVE about a\n   134\t  neurosymbolic system whose hypothesis is a *profile* change (D4 up, D2 flat).\n   135\t  This is independent quantitative support for the domain-vector-first rule:\n   136\t  the scalar exists for ranking, the vector for honesty (ASM-0811 (3)), and for\n   137\t  the D4 diagnostics being non-optional — they are the only components not\n   138\t  loading on the legacy common factor. It also licenses item-level suite\n   139\t  compression at R-1 (IRT-selected subsets) as a cost lever IF we accept\n   140\t  reconstruction error as added variance — flagged as a P3-D-INDEX option, not\n   141\t  a default [EXTRAPOLATION].\n   142\t\n   143\t## 3. Open-LLM-Leaderboard-v2: components, and which floor out at 100M–2B\n   144\t\n   145\t- LOAD-BEARING: OLLv2's six components and setups are: IFEval (0-shot, strict\n   146\t  acc, prompt+inst level), BBH (3-shot, acc_norm over 24 subtasks, choices\n   147\t  2–19), MATH Lvl-5 (4-shot, exact match), GPQA (0-shot, acc_norm, 4-choice),\n   148\t  MuSR (0-shot, acc_norm, 3 subtasks: 2/5/3-choice), MMLU-Pro (5-shot, acc,\n   149\t  10-choice); run on the EleutherAI lm-evaluation-harness with chat template\n   150\t  for instruct models [LIT-BACKED:\n   151\t  huggingface.co/docs/leaderboards/open_llm_leaderboard/about, 2024].\n   152\t- Floor evidence at the programme's rungs, from the SmolLM2 instruct cards\n   153\t  (own-harness lighteval; anchor numbers, not our measurements):\n   154\t  - MMLU-Pro MCF: 1.7B-Instruct **19.3** vs 10% chance (10-choice) — ~9 pp of\n   155\t    normalized headroom; Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct 24.2, Llama-1B-Instruct 12.7\n   156\t    (≈ 3 pp above chance) [LIT-BACKED: HuggingFaceTB/SmolLM2-1.7B-Instruct\n   157\t    card, 2025]. At 135M/360M the cards do not even report MMLU-Pro (they\n   158\t    report MMLU-cloze) — the fact-sheet inconsistency stands [LIT-BACKED:\n   159\t    reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md §2 + HuggingFaceTB cards, 2025].\n   160\t  - BBH 3-shot: 1.7B-Instruct **32.2**, 135M-Instruct **28.2** [LIT-BACKED:\n   161\t    SmolLM2 instruct cards, 2025]. Since many BBH subtasks are 2–3 choice\n   162\t    (raw chance on the suite is roughly 25–30% region depending on mix), a\n   163\t    28–32 raw score is at-or-near the per-subtask-normalized FLOOR — the 135M\n   164\t    vs 1.7B gap of 4 raw points confirms BBH barely discriminates ≤2B\n   165\t    [EXTRAPOLATION from the verified num_choices list; exact normalized floor\n   166\t    to be computed mechanically in P3-D-INDEX from the pinned subtask list].\n   167\t  - GSM8K: base cards 1.4 / 3.2 / 31.0 (135M/360M/1.7B, 5-shot); 48.2 at\n   168\t    1.7B-Instruct [LIT-BACKED: reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md §2.9 +\n   169\t    SmolLM2-1.7B-Instruct card, 2025]. Floored at ≤360M; discriminative at\n   170\t    1.7B → D5 enters at R-2 (≥1.7B-class), not R-1, unless the R-1 rung is\n   171\t    ≥1B [STIPULATED].\n   172\t  - IFEval: 135M-Instruct **29.9** (card's prompt/inst average), 1.7B-Instruct\n   173\t    **56.7** [LIT-BACKED: SmolLM2 instruct cards, 2025]. IFEval's floor is ~0\n   174\t    (generative, strict verification), so it is above floor and discriminative\n   175\t    ACROSS the whole 135M–1.7B range — better than the review's \"iff above\n   176\t    floor at R-2\" prior suggested. Caveat: the card averages prompt-level and\n   177\t    instance-level strict accuracy; the pinned harness must pick one (OLLv2\n   178\t    uses both, reported separately) [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2311.07911, 2023 — 25\n   179\t    verifiable instruction types, ~500 prompts, strict/loose × prompt/inst].\n   180\t  - MuSR: \"few models achieve better than random performance\" — the OLLv2\n   180\t  - MuSR: \"few models achieve better than random performance\" — the OLLv2\n   181\t    docs' own words, at ALL scales it hosts [LIT-BACKED: OLLv2 about page,\n   182\t    2024]. Exclude below R-3.\n   183\t  - MATH Lvl-5 and GPQA: at ≤2B these sit at ~0 and ~chance respectively on\n   184\t    the public leaderboard; **UNVERIFIED as specific numbers** (not fetched\n   185\t    per-model this session) — but GSM8K 1.4–31.0 at our rungs bounds MATH\n   186\t    Lvl-5 from above, and GPQA is designed to be expert-hard. Exclude below\n   187\t    R-3 [EXTRAPOLATION].\n   188\t- CONCLUSION (confirms the REVIEW-CITED claim at source): **OLLv2 is the right\n   189\t  R-3+ anchor family and floors out at the programme's 100M–2B rungs** except\n   190\t  IFEval (usable from R-1 with an instruct host) and BBH-as-diagnostic-only.\n   191\t  The rev-2 provisional D1–D7 table's R-3+ placements are correct as written\n   192\t  [STIPULATED].\n   193\t\n   194\t## 4. Contamination: what detection can and cannot do, and how sealed benchmarks are produced\n   195\t\n   196\t**Detection limits (the review's skepticism is literature-confirmed).**\n   197\t\n   198\t- LOAD-BEARING: simple variations of test data — paraphrase, translation —\n   199\t  defeat n-gram/string-match decontamination: a 13B model trained on rephrased\n   200\t  test sets reached GPT-4-level scores on MMLU/GSM8K/HumanEval while passing\n   201\t  standard decontamination; their stronger LLM-based decontaminator then found\n   202\t  8–18% of HumanEval overlapping in RedPajama-1T/StarCoder-Data, and\n   203\t  contamination even inside GPT-3.5/4-generated synthetic data [LIT-BACKED:\n   204\t  Yang, Chiang, Zheng, Gonzalez, Stoica, arXiv:2311.04850, 2023].\n   205\t- LOAD-BEARING: contamination can be PROVEN (not just screened) from black-box\n   206\t  log-probabilities via exchangeability — a contaminated model assigns higher\n   207\t  likelihood to the benchmark's canonical ordering than to shuffles; works down\n   208\t  to 1.4B models, 1,000-example sets, few duplications — but requires logprob\n   209\t  access and duplication-rate knowledge, and a NEGATIVE result proves nothing\n   210\t  [LIT-BACKED: Oren, Meister, Chatterji, Ladhak, Hashimoto, arXiv:2310.17623,\n   211\t  2023 (ICLR 2024 — venue not restated on the arXiv page; UNVERIFIED as venue)].\n   212\t- Design consequence [STIPULATED]: the §2.2 screener (n-gram + embedding) is a\n   213\t  gross-leakage gate ONLY — the literature says paraphrase-level leakage passes\n   214\t  it, which is exactly why the rev-2 design made the sealed evaluation (§2.2a)\n   215\t  mandatory rather than optional. For OUR stores (which we control) the Oren\n   216\t  test is inapplicable (it detects training-data memorization); the screener +\n   217\t  authoring provenance rules carry that side. For DONOR WEIGHTS, the Oren test\n   218\t  is a cheap, runnable diagnostic on any open comparator with logprob access —\n   219\t  add it to the P3-D-SEAL toolbox.\n   220\t\n   221\t**Producing a sealed/refreshed held-out benchmark (three verified recipes).**\n   222\t\n   223\t- LOAD-BEARING (parallel-replica recipe): GSM1k built 1,000 NEW human-written\n   224\t  problems matched to GSM8K on human solve rates, solution step counts, and\n   225\t  answer magnitudes, kept the set private, and measured overfitting as the\n   226\t  GSM8k→GSM1k gap: drops up to 8%, systematic across some model families,\n   227\t  minimal at the frontier, with the gap correlating (Spearman r² = 0.36) with\n   228\t  the model's probability of *generating* GSM8k items verbatim [LIT-BACKED:\n   229\t  Zhang et al., arXiv:2405.00332, NeurIPS 2024 D&B].\n   230\t- LOAD-BEARING (refresh recipe): LiveBench adds/updates questions monthly from\n   231\t  fresh sources (competitions, arXiv, news), scores against objective ground\n   232\t  truth without LLM judges, and versions each release; ICLR 2025 Spotlight\n   233\t  [LIT-BACKED: White et al., arXiv:2406.19314, ICLR 2025]. The live site\n   234\t  fetch returned no content this session (livebench.ai rendered empty to the\n   235\t  fetcher) — current release ID **UNVERIFIED**; pin the release at P3-D-SEAL\n   236\t  freeze from the GitHub tags, which the review itself cited.\n   237\t- LOAD-BEARING (structural-resistance recipe): SWE-bench Pro's held-out set +\n   238\t  strong-copyleft sourcing, verified in the prior fact sheet [LIT-BACKED:\n   239\t  reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md §3.5, arXiv:2509.16941, 2025].\n   240\t- Recipe synthesis for P3-D-SEAL [STIPULATED]: (i) for D4/D5 procedural\n   241\t  domains, a PINNED GENERATOR with fresh seeds per release is the cheapest\n   242\t  sealed producer (CLUTRR is literally built for this — generator +\n   243\t  configurable splits [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1908.06177, EMNLP 2019]); (ii) for\n   244\t  knowledge/instruction domains, GSM1k-style matched authoring with the\n   245\t  difficulty-matching triple (solve rate, step count, answer magnitude) as the\n   246\t  consistency-band instrument; (iii) LiveBench-style versioning discipline —\n   247\t  every refresh is a new version, never folded in (ASM-0812 verbatim). The\n   248\t  GSM1k measured gap (≤8% for honest models) gives the first EMPIRICAL\n   249\t  calibration for the pre-registered frozen-vs-sealed consistency band: a gap\n   250\t  well beyond that range on matched-difficulty items is evidence of\n   251\t  suite-specific adaptation, not noise [EXTRAPOLATION — band constant to be\n   252\t  set in P3-D-SEAL with our own variance estimates].\n   253\t\n   254\t## 5. Proxy-rung validity: when a cheap rung predicts a dearer one\n   255\t\n   256\tThe question P3-D-POWER and the G5 gate need answered: do R-0/R-1 measurements\n   257\tpredict R-2+? The literature's answer is **metric-dependent**, in a way that\n   258\tdirectly shapes which metrics enter low rungs.\n   259\t\n   260\t- LOAD-BEARING: apparent emergence (sharp capability jumps that would make\n   261\t  small-scale results non-predictive) is largely an artifact of nonlinear/\n   262\t  discontinuous metrics (exact match, MC accuracy); under continuous metrics\n   263\t  the same models improve smoothly and predictably [LIT-BACKED: Schaeffer,\n   264\t  Miranda, Koyejo, arXiv:2304.15004, 2023 (NeurIPS 2023 — venue not restated\n   265\t  on the arXiv page)].\n   266\t- LOAD-BEARING: the mechanism for WHY downstream MC accuracy resists scaling\n   267\t  prediction: performance is computed from log-likelihoods through a chain of\n   268\t  transformations (normalize, compare against specific incorrect choices) that\n   269\t  progressively degrades the statistical relationship with scale — probability\n   270\t  mass on the INCORRECT options fluctuates unpredictably [LIT-BACKED:\n   271\t  Schaeffer, Schoelkopf, Biderman et al., arXiv:2406.04391, 2024].\n   272\t- LOAD-BEARING: nevertheless, aggregate capabilities ARE predictable\n   273\t  observationally: ~100 public models define a low-dimensional capability\n   274\t  space in which \"emergent\" phenomena, agent performance, and even the value\n   275\t  of post-training techniques (CoT, self-consistency) follow smooth sigmoids\n   276\t  predictable from smaller/weaker models [LIT-BACKED: Ruan, Maddison,\n   277\t  Hashimoto, arXiv:2405.10938, NeurIPS 2024 spotlight].\n   278\t- Corroborating instance: BBH tasks that looked flat (\"emergent\") under\n   279\t  direct prompting became smoothly improvable with CoT — the metric/method,\n   280\t  not the underlying capability, produced the cliff [LIT-BACKED: Suzgun et\n   281\t  al., arXiv:2210.09261, 2022].\n   282\t- Design consequences [STIPULATED]:\n   283\t  1. **R-0/R-1 components should be continuous or minimal-pair-logprob metrics**\n   284\t     (held-out LM loss; BLiMP/EWoK forced-choice logprob), which carry\n   285\t     predictive signal upward; MC accuracy at a rung where the model is near\n   286\t     floor carries almost none. This retroactively validates rev-2's R-0 row\n   287\t     (LM loss + BLiMP/EWoK) and the MMLU-cloze drop.\n   288\t  2. **Proxy-rung validity must be CLAIMED per metric family, not per suite**:\n   289\t     the G5 two-size confirmation should test the margin's direction on the\n   290\t     continuous metrics first, and treat cross-rung MC-accuracy extrapolation\n   291\t     as unsupported by default.\n   292\t  3. For P3-D-POWER: Δ_max computations at R-1 on near-floor MC benchmarks are\n   293\t     unstable (the baseline accuracy term a_b^cov is noise near chance) — the\n   294\t     power table should exclude below-floor benchmarks from κ-weighted gain\n   295\t     projections rather than average them in [STIPULATED].\n   296\t\n   297\t## 6. Per-benchmark floors/ceilings at tiny scale (the D1–D7 evidence table)\n   298\t\n   299\tThe rev-2 provisional domain table (programme doc §1.1), now annotated with\n   300\tverified floors/ceilings. \"Tiny scale\" = the programme's 135M–1.7B hosts (and,\n   301\tfor D1, the BabyLM ≤100M-word training regime as the closest published probe\n   302\tof very small capability).\n   303\t\n   304\t| Benchmark | Verified construction | Floor/ceiling at tiny scale | Verdict for the index |\n   305\t|---|---|---|---|\n   306\t| **BLiMP** | 67 paradigms × 1,000 minimal pairs, forced-choice by sentence logprob, chance 50% [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1912.00582, TACL 2020] | GPT-2 81.5 / LSTM 69.8 / 5-gram 61.2 / human 88.6 individual, 96.4 aggregate [LIT-BACKED: aclanthology.org/2020.tacl-1.25 via paper PDF]; BabyLM-2024 10M-word baselines 69.8, best 81.2; 100M-word best 86.1 [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2412.05149, CoNLL 2024] | **KEEP at R-0/R-1 (D1)**: discriminative across the whole tiny range, ceiling pinnable at human 88.6/96.4 |\n   307\t| **EWoK** | 4,374 items, 11 domains, minimal pairs-of-pairs (context×target), chance 50%, logprob-scored [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2405.09605, TACL 2025 + ewok-core pages] | All tested 1.3B–70B models below human; social domains highest, physical/spatial lowest [LIT-BACKED: same]; BabyLM-2024: baselines 50.7–51.9 (≈chance), best submission 58.4 — \"most submissions perform near chance\" [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:2412.05149] | **KEEP at R-0 with a floor warning**: at ≤100M-word training it is nearly floored; systematic (held-out rule combinations) + robustness splits; text vs symbolic-graph input arms [LIT-BACKED: arXiv:1908.06177, EMNLP 2019; licence CC-BY-NC-4.0 per reports/lit-eval-benchmarks.md] | Paper-era: BERT/MAC on text ≪ GAT on symbolic graphs for generalization+robustness [LIT-BACKED: same]. Use\n    25\t`date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ` in bash for the timestamp (a script cannot stamp\n    26\titself).\n    27\t\n    28\t## (2) Reproducibility\n    29\t\n    30\tThe experiment is driven by a SINGLE committed script that runs the whole\n    31\tthing end-to-end with all inputs pinned (frozen record sha + corpus + model\n    32\tids) and all outputs going to declared paths (results-log + verdict). A Fable\n    33\tagent must be able to re-run that one script and audit inputs -> outputs. For\n    34\tf2b-replicate the script is `poc/f2/run-f2b-replicate.sh`.\n    35\t\n    36\t## (3) Audit-tracking\n    37\t\n    38\t`registry/audit-status.jsonl` carries one JSON line per experiment:\n    39\t\n    40\t```\n    41\t{experiment_id, executed_by:\"opus\"|\"fable\", executor_model,\n    42\t codex_audited:\"CONFIRM\"|\"REFUTE\"|\"pending\"|\"n/a\",\n    43\t fable_interpretive_assessed:\"pending\"|\"done\",\n    44\t run_log_path, run_script_path, verdict_path, note}\n    45\t```\n    46\t\n    47\tThis tracks which experiments have — and have NOT — had a FABLE interpretive\n    48\tassessment. `tools/registry/audit-status.py` lists the experiments and flags\n    49\tthose still awaiting a Fable interpretive assessment.\n    50\t\n    51\t## (4) No conclusive analysis by Opus\n    52\t\n    53\tAn Opus agent NEVER writes, saves, or commits a conclusive interpretation of an\n    54\toutcome. Two outputs ARE allowed because they are deterministic or\n    55\tcross-vendor, not an Opus analysis:\n    56\t\n    57\t- the mechanical verdict-gen output (a pure function of the frozen rules over\n    58\t  the run records);\n    59\t- the Codex / GPT-5.5 cross-vendor audit.\n    60\t\n    61\tThe INTERPRETIVE assessment — what the outcome MEANS, the kill-chain read, any\n    62\tEXTRAPOLATION -> MEASURED promotion, the kot-assess narrative — WAITS for Fable\n    63\tand is marked pending. Opus reports only the mechanical numbers and the\n    64\tpre-declared gate pass/fails (which are pure functions), never a narrative\n    65\tconclusion.\n    66\t\n    67\t## (5) Pre-spend reuse gate (BINDING — revision-1, post-Codex-audit)\n    68\t\n    69\tBefore ANY GPU/paid launch, the Runner runs the gate — `--gate` is MANDATORY\n    70\ton BOTH forms (without it the tool is discovery-only and licenses nothing):\n    71\t\n    72\t```\n    73\tpython3 tools/registry/reuse-check.py check --record registry/experiments/<id>.json --gate\n    74\tpython3 tools/registry/reuse-check.py check --arm <arm> --rung <rung> [--corpus <c>] --gate\n    75\t```\n    76\t\n    77\tand records the full output in the run-log. P2 G-11 and P6 §4; maintainer confirms at I-BUDGET): Tier 0 ≤ $20 (API only) · Tier 1 ≤ $80 · Tier 2 ≤ $400 · Tier 3 ≤ $400 · cumulative Tiers 0–3 ≤ $900 · Tier 4 (F5) ≤ $900 · Tier 5 = maintainer-approved envelope ($2–10k). Worst-case Tiers 0–3 ≈ $760 < $900. **Freeze-time tier-sum lint:** Σ(frozen worst-cases in a tier) ≤ tier cap — `prereg-freeze` refuses a freeze that would break the sum, so a fully-utilised tier can never BUDGET-HALT mid-campaign by arithmetic. | Pre-launch: harness refuses any RunSpec whose worst_case_usd exceeds remaining tier headroom (`ERR_BUDGET`). In-flight: hourly cost monitor polls Modal spend + ledger; breach ⇒ **kill switch**: `modal app stop` on every live app in the tier + `poc/gpu/terminate.sh` for any AWS boxes + BUDGET-HALT status event in the log (P2 G-11) + bead filed + maintainer notified. Every container additionally carries its sized hard timeout (a wedged run self-caps). Positives get full audits; kills/nulls get conformance audits. The paper gets the same treatment (paper.review). poc/gpt56-review/p3-review-20260711/events.jsonl:27:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_14","type":"agent_message","text":"## Overall judgement\n\nThe programme is intellectually serious, unusually candid about its weak evidence base, and worth continuing—but the current design should be revised before it is frozen or converted into experiment beads.\n\nThe architecture literature reviews can begin now. The measurement framework, dependency graph, and several falsifiers should be redesigned first. As written, a W1 “win” could be statistically real yet still depend on resource-band gaming, a narrow and correlated benchmark index, unmatched lifecycle investment, or an inadequately strong neural/RAG baseline.\n\n## 1. Measurement and fair comparison\n\n### What is strong\n\nThe framework in [§1–2](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/programme-3-neurosymbolic-architecture.md:96>) gets several important things right:\n\n- It measures the whole deployed system, not just neural parameters.\n- It freezes stores and indices and includes them in the artifact.\n- It evaluates natural benchmark inputs rather than hand-formalized versions.\n- It requires own-harness baselines, full score vectors, abstention reporting, decontamination, and aligned-store controls.\n- It separates the competitive system claim from kernel-specific attribution.\n- It proposes calibration on neural models before using the instrument.\n\nThose are substantive strengths, not cosmetic safeguards.\n\n### The ±10% matching rule is gameable\n\nBilateral ±10% bands are not the right primary comparison. They permit:\n\n- Padding an artifact to enter a more favorable model band.\n- Avoiding a strong model just outside the band.\n- Having no credible comparator in the intersection of size and compute bands.\n- Calling a system competitive when a smaller or cheaper neural system already dominates it.\n\nReplace bands with a resource-constrained frontier:\n\n> At resource budget `(maximum deployment bytes, maximum latency/energy/memory)`, compare S against the best neural or neural-retrieval system that fits within every budget.\n\nThe comparator should be allowed to be smaller or cheaper, not merely within ±10%. Report interpolation or a staircase envelope across neural Pareto points. W1 should mean that S lies significantly above the best baseline capability frontier, not that it won a favorable neighborhood.\n\nThe claim should also be narrowed from “all other models” to “all pre-registered, reproducible open comparators and baseline families searched under the specified budget.” The literal universal claim is not testable.\n\n### “Same compute” is underspecified and partly mislabeled\n\nKOT-COST/1’s neural-FLOP ledger assigns symbolic work zero. That can be a useful diagnostic—“neural FLOPs”—but it is not a compute ledger. A system performing substantial CPU search, retrieval, decompression, or I/O can appear compute-matched there by definition.\n\nUse a resource vector:\n\n- accelerator time and estimated operations;\n- CPU-seconds;\n- bytes read from storage and network;\n- energy;\n- peak GPU memory and host RSS;\n- end-to-end latency;\n- throughput under a fixed concurrency distribution.\n\nW1 currently mentions wall-clock and memory but omits the energy constraint even though energy is supposedly part of KOT-COST/1. That inconsistency must be fixed.\n\nMeasure both warm- and cold-cache behavior, startup/index-load time, p50/p95 latency, throughput, time to first token, and inter-token latency. Output length must be controlled or cost normalized appropriately. MLPerf uses explicit latency, throughput, compliance, and full-system power methodology for exactly these reasons; the programme should borrow that measurement discipline rather than relying on query-level `nvidia-smi` readings alone. [MLPerf inference methodology](https://mlcommons.org/benchmarks/inference-datacenter/), [MLPerf power measurement](https://docs.mlcommons.org/inference/power/).\n\n### Deployment size is useful, but not sufficient\n\nRaw on-disk bytes are a defensible requested metric, provided packaging is canonical. Otherwise serialization format, padding, duplicate metadata, compression settings, and what gets moved into the “common base image” are gaming surfaces.\n\nReport at least:\n\n1. Canonically packed, minimally sufficient artifact bytes.\n2. Compressed distribution bytes.\n3. Warm resident RAM/VRAM.\n4. Cold-start working set and bytes read.\n5. Index/store construction size and cost.\n6. Any remote service, cache, or corpus dependency.\n\nFreeze the common base image before architecture development. Otherwise custom functionality can migrate into the supposedly free base.\n\nMost importantly, add a lifecycle ledger. The current design explicitly compares deployment efficiency, not equal total compute. That is legitimate, but it must be named accurately. Report:\n\n- donor pretraining provenance where known;\n- architecture fine-tuning and hyperparameter-search compute;\n- store authoring, parsing, embedding, indexing, and human-review costs;\n- total cost amortized at several deployment volumes, such as \\(10^3, 10^6,\\) and \\(10^9\\) queries.\n\nTraining longer to obtain a smaller model is a real deployment strategy, and optimal model size depends on expected inference volume. [Sardana et al., “Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal”](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v235/sardana24a.html).\n\n### Baselines need strengthening\n\nThe strongest missing control is:\n\n> Same corpus, same retriever/index, same resource budgets, but a conventional neural RAG or tool-use architecture.\n\nThe aligned-non-kernel store does not replace this. The programme must distinguish:\n\n- kernel semantics;\n- structured storage;\n- retrieval;\n- deterministic execution;\n- retry/search;\n- specialized neural-symbolic integration.\n\nBaseline optimization should include quantization, structured pruning, distillation, retrieval, and compute-optimal sampling or verification. Best-of-\\(k\\) self-consistency alone is too weak: adaptive test-time compute can substantially outperform naive best-of-\\(N\\). [ICLR 2025 test-time-compute study](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/hash/1b623663fd9b874366f3ce019fdfdd44-Abstract-Conference.html).\n\nEqual numbers of hyperparameter configurations are also not sufficient symmetry because configurations differ greatly in cost. Match total tuning compute and use fixed selection rules.\n\n### Decontamination is necessary but cannot certify fairness\n\nN-gram and embedding overlap screening can catch gross answer-key leakage, but not:\n\n- benchmark source knowledge encoded as rules or abstractions;\n- selective store authoring based on benchmark error analysis;\n- paraphrases below the threshold;\n- benchmark contamination in donor weights;\n- repeated researcher adaptation to the frozen test suite.\n\nAdd a sealed evaluation component produced after architecture and store freeze, preferably by an independent party or procedural generator. For store-based systems, include temporally fresh facts or independently held-out domains.\n\nUse a factorial control design: content type × retrieval architecture × executor. Derangement alone is weak because it changes alignment and often retrieval behavior simultaneously. Include label permutation, irrelevant-but-structurally-matched records, edge shuffling, and matched generic RAG.\n\n### Statistical specification needs correction\n\nW1 should require the lower confidence bound on the difference to exceed the practical margin:\n\n\\[\nLCB_{95}(INDEX(S)-INDEX(B)) > \\delta_k\n\\]\n\nA point estimate above \\(\\delta_k\\) plus an LCB merely above zero does not establish the margin.\n\nBecause S is tested against multiple baselines, use simultaneous confidence bounds or family-wise error control. Bootstrap hierarchically across benchmark families and items, preserving paired predictions.\n\n“TOST non-inferiority” is technically incorrect. TOST is normally an equivalence procedure with two one-sided tests. A non-inferiority claim uses the appropriate single one-sided test or confidence bound against the non-inferiority margin.\n\n## The AI index\n\nThe current nine-task suite is reasonable for SmolLM2 harness calibration. It is not adequate as the headline “AI-capability index.”\n\nSeven components are closely related multiple-choice commonsense or factual tasks. An unweighted macro-average therefore overweights that construct while underrepresenting:\n\n- language modeling and linguistic competence;\n- instruction following;\n- natural-language generation;\n- compositional and relational generalization;\n- logical reasoning;\n- calibration and selective prediction;\n- multilingual performance;\n- coding below R-3;\n- robustness under distribution shift.\n\nThe raw average also combines binary, four-way, five-way, and open-ended tasks with different chance floors and variances. Normalize scores relative to chance and a pinned ceiling, then macro-average first within capability domains and only then across domains. Publish both the domain vector and the scalar.\n\nMMLU-cloze is particularly problematic: the repository itself notes inconsistent MMLU variants across SmolLM2 sizes. It should not be silently treated as a stable cross-rung component.\n\nRecommended structure:\n\n- **R-0:** held-out language-model loss plus BabyLM-style BLiMP/EWoK linguistic evaluation; small procedural relational and rule-reasoning suites.\n- **R-1/R-2 general capability:** a reduced set from HellaSwag, ARC-Challenge, PIQA, WinoGrande, MMLU, GSM8K, plus instruction following where above floor.\n- **Neurosymbolic diagnostics:** CLUTRR, ProofWriter/RuleTaker, FOLIO or similar, with held-out rules, relation compositions, proof depths, and lexical paraphrases. CLUTRR explicitly evaluates systematic relational generalization and includes natural-language graph construction, making it especially relevant. [CLUTRR paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.06177).\n- **R-3+:** MMLU-Pro, BBH or BBEH where discriminative, IFEval, code, and a frozen release of LiveBench or another recent objective benchmark.\n- **Contamination-resistant secondary:** a sealed or periodically refreshed suite. LiveBench is useful because it refreshes questions and uses objective scoring, though its changing nature means each release must be versioned rather than folded silently into a fixed index. [LiveBench](https://github.com/livebench/livebench).\n\nHELM’s multi-scenario, multi-metric structure is a better conceptual model than a flat accuracy average. [Stanford HELM](https://nlp.stanford.edu/helm/vhelm/). The Open LLM Leaderboard v2 suite is suitable at larger scales but the document is right that much of it may floor out at tiny scales. [Official benchmark descriptions](https://huggingface.co/docs/leaderboards/main/open_llm_leaderboard/about).\n\nKeep the current suite as “SmolLM2 continuity index,” not as the sole capability index.\n\n## 2. Architecture bets\n\n### Verifier loop\n\nThis is the best-motivated family because it has the only measured system-level positive shape. The proposed index-level falsifier is sensible, provided it compares against:\n\n- compute-optimal neural sampling;\n- a learned neural verifier;\n- generic structured RAG;\n- grammar/constrained decoding;\n- the same verifier with a non-kernel store.\n\nRun a coverage upper-bound calculation first:\n\n\\[\n\\Delta_{\\text{maximum index}} \\leq \\sum_b w_b \\kappa_b\n(1-\\text{baseline accuracy on covered items})\n\\]\n\nIf even perfect covered-item correction cannot reach \\(\\delta\\), kill the index-mover claim without GPU work.\n\n### GNN fusion\n\nThe hypothesis is plausible, particularly for relational composition and systematic generalization. But “GNN ≤ text serialization” at R-0/R-1 does not kill the entire family. Text is often superior for tiny graphs, while graph encoders may become useful only as graph size and reasoning depth grow.\n\nControls should include:\n\n- same retrieved nodes with no edges;\n- shuffled edges and relation labels;\n- MLP/DeepSets/Transformer encoders with the same inputs;\n- text serialization at equal information and resource budgets;\n- held-out relation compositions and proof depths;\n- oracle graph versus NL-extracted graph.\n\nSeparate four stages: retrieval, graph construction, graph encoding, and LM use. A positive delivery probe is insufficient; require causal behavioral improvement.\n\n### Rule placement\n\nThe cheap-first order CD → KV → adapters/activations → hidden layer is sensible. Several claims need tightening:\n\n- Constrained decoding does not itself perform inference; it enforces an output language unless the external engine has already derived the valid continuation set.\n- Attention weights are not adequate provenance evidence. KV provenance requires causal ablation, intervention, or executor traces.\n- A dedicated concept bottleneck is not strongly interpretable merely because its units are named. Provenance must be causally validated.\n- Cost parity with the external loop should not be the only endpoint. An internal mechanism might be worthwhile if it improves accuracy, coverage, or latency variance. Compare Pareto frontiers.\n\nA promising missing design is explicit NL-to-program synthesis: parse into a small executable DSL, execute deterministically, and generate from the checked result with calibrated abstention. That attacks the actual boundary problem more directly than hoping rule knowledge emerges in a hidden layer.\n\n### Ground-up training\n\nA matched twin is correct, but one R-0 failure should not imply failure at R-2. Interfaces may require enough model capacity to become usable. Run at least three small widths or token budgets and test the direction of scaling.\n\nThe most promising training variants missing are:\n\n- distillation from symbolic proof traces;\n- verifier-guided training rather than verifier-only retry;\n- synthetic curriculum over increasing rule depth and paraphrase diversity;\n- learned routing so the symbolic path is activated selectively;\n- conditional-compute or MoE designs with a symbolic expert.\n\n### Dimension drop\n\nThis is currently the weakest technical component.\n\nAn arbitrary per-layer orthogonal rotation is not generally function-preserving through elementwise nonlinearities, normalization, residual connections, attention head structure, and tied embeddings merely because adjacent linear maps are adjusted. Some restricted residual-stream basis changes can be absorbed, but the exact invariance group must be derived for the chosen architecture.\n\nSAE features are typically overcomplete and non-orthogonal; matching them to concepts does not produce a removable orthogonal coordinate block.\n\nAlso, dropping 80% of hidden width does not imply a 5× smaller dense transformer. Major matrices scale approximately quadratically with width, so the reduction could be much larger—while embeddings and other components scale differently. Conversely, “dropping 80% of dimensions” could mean rank or parameter pruning, in which case 5× needs an explicit parameterization.\n\nBefore the five-arm full experiment, add a cheaper causal gate:\n\n1. Identify a purported concept subspace.\n2. Ablate or patch it on concept-relevant and irrelevant tasks.\n3. Test stability across layers, prompts, and donors.\n4. Show that store reinjection specifically restores the lost behavior.\n5. Only then attempt structural width pruning and distillation.\n\nCompare against the strongest retrained structured-pruning and knowledge-distillation baselines, not pruning without recovery training.\n\n## 3. Scaling ladder and cost approach\n\nThe cheap-first intent is good, but the ladder should be reorganized around information-gaining gates:\n\n1. **Coverage ceiling:** Can perfect oracle use move the index by \\(\\delta\\)?\n2. **Oracle interface:** With gold parses/subgraphs, does the architecture help?\n3. **Boundary degradation:** How much survives realistic NL mapping?\n4. **Full system:** Does it beat the resource-constrained baseline frontier?\n5. **Scaling:** Does the margin improve across at least two sizes?\n\nThis kills failures earlier and localizes them. Presently, an architecture can fail because the graph encoder is bad, retrieval is bad, or NL parsing is bad, with no clean diagnosis.\n\nDo not require every experiment, including NLB, to wait for index calibration. NLB and coverage work can proceed independently. Conversely, every store-touching natural-input experiment—not only H-VL—must explicitly depend on NLB or be labeled an oracle/formal diagnostic.\n\nThere is also a W2 inconsistency: P3-E-SCALE-2 promises a Pareto sweep, while §4 says W2 begins only after W1. Make the R-2 sweep conditional on W1, or label earlier compression curves diagnostic rather than W2.\n\n## 4. Work breakdown\n\nThe broad literature-first structure is good, but add or expand these reviews:\n\n- RAG, structured retrieval, knowledge graphs, and tool-using neural baselines.\n- Semantic parsing, program synthesis, grammar induction, uncertainty, and selective prediction.\n- Neural theorem proving, differentiable logic, neural algorithmic reasoning, and proof-trace distillation.\n- Causal representation analysis and the exact invariances of transformer model surgery.\n- Lifecycle cost, systems benchmarking, storage/I/O, and MLPerf-style measurement.\n- Benchmark contamination, sealed evaluation, composite-index statistics, and proxy-rung validity.\n- Store authoring, maintenance, provenance, and knowledge-update economics.\n\nMissing design tasks include:\n\n- a formal resource/baseline threat model;\n- a neural frontier-builder that optimizes baselines under each budget;\n- a coverage × maximum-gain power analysis;\n- an independent sealed benchmark;\n- a matched generic-RAG control;\n- oracle-to-NL error decomposition;\n- hardware repeatability and cold/warm-cache protocols;\n- lifecycle cost amortization.\n\nP3-D-NLB should itself depend on a semantic-parsing/selective-prediction review. The current “no blocker” status risks repeating the already-measured parser failure without first reconsidering the approach.\n\n## 5. Honesty and programme risks\n\nThe document is commendably honest about the current evidence. It accurately carries forward the central result from [feasibility synthesis §0–2](</home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/feasibility-synthesis.md:33>): exact machinery inside a closed grammar, but 47.6% and 41.6% retention across the two NL boundaries, one with dangerous wrong answers, plus extremely low external coverage.\n\nHowever, it overstates one conclusion in §7.3: the comparison has not yet been shown capable of being “made fair.” It has been made auditable in several respects, but unresolved training-data contamination, lifecycle cost, baseline search, and artifact-band gaming remain.\n\nThe natural-language failure is acknowledged rhetorically but not fully encoded in dependencies. GNN retrieval, KV rules, constrained decoding, and H-DD store reinjection all need query-to-symbol grounding. NLB must gate all natural-input store use, or experiments must explicitly use oracle inputs and make no W1 claim.\n\nThe programme-level kill is too soft and too subjective. “Analysts attribute misses to fundamentals rather than instrument failure” leaves a wide escape hatch. Pre-register quantitative kills:\n\n- perfect-oracle coverage cannot reach \\(\\delta\\);\n- NLB fails safety or minimum precision/recall after a fixed number of redesigns;\n- no family exceeds the neural/RAG frontier at R-1 and R-2;\n- gains disappear against matched generic stores;\n- integration gains do not survive natural input;\n- total lifecycle costs are dominated at all plausible deployment volumes.\n\nIf none passes W1 at R-2, the default should be terminate the competitiveness programme, with a new proposal required to restart—not an open-ended maintainer fork.\n\n## Ranked concerns\n\n1. **The comparison frontier is wrong.** ±10% size/compute bands are gameable and do not establish dominance over resource-constrained neural and RAG alternatives.\n2. **The headline index is too narrow and statistically ill-formed.** It overweights correlated legacy multiple-choice benchmarks and averages incomparable raw scores.\n3. **The NL/coverage wall is not enforced in the experiment dependencies.** Several architectures can reach Phase 2 without demonstrating usable query-to-symbol grounding.\n4. **H-DD contains unsupported and partly incorrect model-surgery assumptions.** Rotation invariance, semantic axis alignment, store replacement, and the 80%→5× relation all need reformulation.\n5. **The baseline and accounting scope is incomplete.** Generic RAG/tool use, optimized test-time compute, store construction, training, and lifecycle economics are not adequately matched.\n\n## Factual or technical errors\n\n- TOST is described as a non-inferiority test; non-inferiority normally uses one one-sided test or CI, while TOST establishes equivalence.\n- A point estimate above \\(\\delta\\) with an LCB above zero does not establish a margin-\\(\\delta\\) win.\n- Arbitrary per-layer orthogonal rotation is not generally function-preserving in a transformer.\n- SAE features do not normally form an orthogonal removable basis.\n- Dropping 80% of hidden dimensions does not generically equal a 5× size reduction.\n- Attention weights do not provide reliable causal provenance.\n- A neural-FLOP ledger in which symbolic computation is zero is not a total-compute measure.\n- Constrained decoding is not rule inference unless an executor has already computed the constraints.\n\n## Recommendation\n\nProceed with the literature reviews, especially evaluation, RAG baselines, semantic parsing, and compression/model surgery. Do not freeze KOT-FAIR/1, bead the current Phase-2 graph, or treat W1 as publication-ready until the resource-frontier comparison, index, lifecycle ledger, sealed evaluation, and NLB dependencies are revised.\n\nThe programme is promising enough to investigate, but not methodologically sound enough to execute as currently specified."}}
